data.title: Understanding Palantir With A Little Help From Ben Graham (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
For The Record

I believe it's worth mentioning, as a prologue to my central message to you today, that I have been a huge believer in Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) since its direct listing.

The stock is up nearly 800% in my LASR Index, and it is, to be sure, still a 4% position, even after the huge selling I did earlier this year.

I believe in the business, its mission, and its leadership, and, today, I am in no sense disputing that Palantir is a brilliant business that's growing rapidly while generating huge free cash flow.

In my view, Palantir is one of the best, most important businesses on earth. Palantir stock and its valuation aside, I could not be more bullish on the company that is Palantir.

With that in mind, let's begin exploring Palantir's valuation today.

Same Stock Market, Different Year

Throughout the history of the modern stock market, dating back to Jonathan's Coffee House in the 17th century, financial bubbles have formed and subsequently deflated, resulting in significant financial loss.

It's logical to think that, following the disasters of the original financial bubbles, such as the South Sea Bubble, investors of those early eras would have learned lessons that they then passed on to the next generation of investors.

Equipped with the lessons of the past, most would likely expect that subsequent generations of investors would avoid investing in and inadvertently creating themselves financial bubbles.

This is, unfortunately, not how the market works in practice, and, despite countless financial bubbles forming over the last 350 years or so of stock market history, new financial bubbles form almost yearly.

To be sure, previous generations of investors have actually gone to extraordinary lengths to teach subsequent generations about the dangers of financial bubbles. These teachings have been codified in some of the most important financial texts in history, such as Ben Graham's Security Analysis.

Below, I shared an excerpt from Ben Graham's Security Analysis, in which he articulated the prevailing view of stock valuations in the 1920s, and thereby attempted to teach subsequent generations of investors about the dangers of investing in overvalued stocks, e.g., stocks that could be characterized as having become financial bubbles.

Stocks Regarded As Attractive Irrespective Of Their Prices

In 1934, Ben Graham published Security Analysis following the Great Depression and the associated stock market collapse.

What's so interesting to me is that the language he cited as being dangerous 92 years ago is the same language that often permeates the consciousness of market participants today.

In fact, in the midst of modern financial bubbles, we often read this language exactly, i.e., investors say we've entered a "new-era" for valuations or "just buy the leaders" and don't worry about valuation.

While there is certainly value in "buying the leader" within a given industry, taken to an extreme, it's invariably a strategy destined to fail.

Today, I read a comment in the comments section of the latest Palantir news in which the author of the comment shared that we should get rid of the price to sales valuation metric as it is no longer useful. The commenter shared that traditional valuation philosophies should be discarded, as they're not valuable in understanding Palantir.

Of course, because we are students of stock market history, we know that this is a misguided way of thinking, and, in fact, has been used as a justification for financial bubbles and their associated speculative behavior since the dawn of the stock market.

There have been many financial bubbles throughout the centuries, and, almost certainly, the same language that Ben Graham cited and which I witnessed today in relation to Palantir was uttered.

For instance, there was Railway Mania in the mid-19th century, at the dawn of the development of the railway transportation network in the U.S. and the Dot Com Bubble, at the dawn of the modern Internet.

RailWay Mania Inflates & Subsequently Deflates

Dot Com Bubble Inflates & Deflates

It's worth noting that financial bubbles arise fairly frequently.

We've discussed three prominent examples, e.g., the conclusion of the Roaring Twenties, Railway Mania, and the Dot Com Bubble; however, financial bubbles are actually a pretty routine occurrence in the stock market.

For instance, in the last 20 years, there have been two fairly noteworthy green energy bubbles: One in the 2000s and one in the early 2020s.

The First Alternative Energy Bubble (Renewable Energy Industrial Index: RENIXX)

There have been two noteworthy 3D printing bubbles: one in the 2010s and one in the 2020s.

3D Printing Bubble Of The 2010s & Early 2020s

There has been at least one cannabis bubble in the last decade.

And, of course, there was the software bubble of the early 2020s, which we'll lean on to understand Palantir's current valuation in just a moment.

Today, we're almost certainly in the midst of a quantum computing bubble. It's actually been pretty wild to witness the degree of speculation there. There are presently companies trading for billions of dollars, with, in one case, a five figure revenue tally for 2025.

In short, financial bubbles do arise, and, interestingly, their characteristics are almost always identical to past bubbles, e.g., market participants begin saying things like, "Past valuations don't apply in today's market. We're in a new-era."

The notion that the desirability of a common stock was entirely independent of its price seems incredibly absurd. Yet the new-era theory led directly to this thesis.

Ben Graham, Security Analysis.

I mean I read nearly this exact language today in relation to Palantir... 92 years after this language was used to justify the excesses of the Roaring Twenties.

To close this section, valuations matter. They are as real and tangible as the laws of physics themselves. For the sake of brevity, I won't delve into the specific mechanics underlying the value of a stock, i.e., the four points of stock value; however, it's worth noting these ideas for our situational awareness.

We Already Know How This Ends

Understanding what will come of Palantir's stock in the next five years is fairly easy.

It's easy because the aftermath of today's Palantir exuberance has been playing out for the last five years via the stock of Palantir's closest peer, Snowflake (SNOW).

With five years of data following Snowflake's exuberant bout of pricing in 2020 and 2021, we can reasonably assume what will transpire for Palantir in the years ahead.

In 2020, Snowflake's narrative and valuation were virtually identical to Palantir's narrative and valuation today.

Snowflake was The Data Super Cloud.

Warren Buffett bought in. Frank Slootman, the man who built one of the greatest software businesses of all time, ServiceNow (NOW), was taking Snowflake to the next level.

It was the data stock to own.

Using this logic, investors bid Snowflake's stock to incredible heights, bringing its valuation to 150-200x sales in late 2020.

Snowflake's EV/Sales And Revenue Growth Rate

In the eyes of the market, Snowflake was the big winner of the data cloud market. As I mentioned, it was the Data Super Cloud, according to the software investing community at the time.

Palantir, as an aside, was the weaker, less well-executed data business with a sketchy management team, so the narrative went. As we know, these two narratives have very ironically flipped, and the extremes have now gone in the complete opposite direction.

From the point in late 2020 in which Snowflake was seen as the Data Super Cloud to today, Snowflake's stock price has fallen 50% or so, down from $300/share to $200/share.

The Data Super Cloud, so it was called in 2020 and 2021, has generated -50% returns over five years.

The casual observer might think, based on this stock price performance: "Oh, well growth must have been poor. The business probably didn't do as well as market participants expected. The thesis didn't work out."

And that casual observer would be wrong.

In fact, Snowflake hasn't just performed well from late 2020. It's performed unbelievably well.

Its performance has been simply breathtaking. Off the charts great.

In 2020, Snowflake grew 123%.

In 2021, it grew 106%.

In 2022, it grew 69%: From over ~$1.2B in sales to ~$2.1B in sales. Incredible growth at huge scale.

In 2023, it grew 36%.

In 2024 and currently, it's sustained ~30% growth (no deceleration in Q1'25 (CY)) at giant scale.

Snowflake has been one of the best businesses in the entire stock market for the last five years.

Period.

It's grown its sales roughly 10x in just about five years.

Snowflake Roughly 10x'd Its Sales From 2020 To 2025

Staggeringly great performance.

This is growth that software investors may not witness for decades to come for a public software business.

I mean I cannot overstate how phenomenal this level of growth has been and how phenomenal Snowflake has performed as a business.

And its stock has declined 50%!

900-1,000% profitable revenue growth in five years.

...

50% stock price decline.

So we're left with the question: How could a business that just grew high margin, free cash flow generative sales at an almost unbelievable rate for five straight years generate -50% returns in that same period?

In short, valuations matter a lot.

There is no new-era. There never will be a new-era. There is a rigidly defined valuation standard that is quite literally coded into the laws of physics.

Turning to Palantir, which is now seen as a transcendental entity for whom valuation does not matter (so the narrative has gone recently).

Today, it trades at well over 100x sales on a fully diluted basis, while growing at ~40%.

Palantir's EV/Sales & Quarterly Revenue Growth Rate

Palantir diluted shareholders by ~6% in 2024, and, should it dilute by 5% in 2025, it will have about 2.7B shares outstanding by the end of 2025. 2.7B times $130/share = $350B in market cap today.

On a trailing twelve months basis, Palantir has generated about $3.3B in sales.

So we're well over 100x sales alongside ~40% growth.

First quarter US commercial revenue grew 71% year-over-year and 19% sequentially to $255 million. Excluding revenue from strategic commercial contracts, first quarter US commercial revenue grew 75% year-over-year and 22% sequentially.

Dave Glazer, CFO, Palantir Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

Palantir U.S. Commercial Revenue Growth Rate

It's -- the reality of what's going on is that this is a unvarnished, cacophony of the combination of 20 years of investment and a massive cultural shift in the US, which is generating numbers. Again, 71% growth in US commercial, 71%. We had journalists this morning that were asking me if it's a seven handle. It 71% growth.

Alex Karp, CEO, Palantir Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

Recall that Snowflake traded at this valuation in 2020 with 150% growth, then 106% growth, then 69% growth, and it has sustained ~30% growth for the last three years on average.

In response to which Snowflake's stock has declined by 50%.

With all due respect to CEO Alex Karp, in whom I've long believed and whose non-linear thinking I've long deeply appreciated, 71% growth is simply not enough to justify 100x+ sales.

Snowflake grew at faster rates at this scale, and it was still not enough to justify its valuation.

Snowflake Grew At 110% At $312.5M Revenue Scale (Palantir Just Grew At 75% At $255M Revenue Scale)

Recall:

900%-1,000% profitable revenue growth from 100x+ sales in late 2020 = 50% stock price decline for Snowflake over the last five years.

And, today, at 18x sales and $205/share, while I am bullish on Snowflake, it's by no means the most obvious buy I've ever come across. Snowflake will need to sustain 25-30% growth for years to come, which is extremely challenging for a business operating at $4B in revenue scale in a market in which there are, at least, several viable competitors, two of which are Palantir and Databricks.

Clickhouse is another rapidly growing and interesting data cloud who just raised money at $6B in valuation from Bezos Expeditions, and Clickhouse is partially owned by the very bright Nebius (NBIS) team.

Like Snowflake of 2020, when it was called Data Super Cloud, at Palantir's current valuation ($130/share), Palantir could layer on huge chunks of revenue and still experience either a stagnating stock for a half decade or a share price decline. Palantir could sustain 50% annualized growth for the next five years: An almost impossible feat of growth for a $3.5B revenue business, which is smaller than its data cloud competitors, e.g., Databricks and Snowflake, and Palantir would still likely not be an obvious buy, akin to what Snowflake has become today after ~10x'ing its revenue over the last 5 years.

We could imagine the following scenario:

  • Palantir sustains 50% annualized growth for the next five years. Unbelievable level of scaling.
  • It reaches $24.3B in sales via the 50% annualized growth for five consecutive years.
  • It generates 35% free cash flow margins on those sales (top tier software free cash flow margins).
  • It generates $8.5B in free cash flow.
  • It trades at 50x fcf (a lofty valuation).
  • And it would trade at $425B in market cap, up 21% at the end of five years.

This is an incredibly, incredibly optimistic scenario, and it's far more likely that Palantir's growth settles in at 20-40% on average over the next five years; principally due to the fact that it does not operate in a market without competitors. As I've mentioned, Databricks and Snowflake are both larger cloud data vendors than Palantir, and Snowflake just sustained its nearly 30% growth rate in Q1'25 (CY). It, in fact, experienced no growth deceleration, indicating that it's effectively competing, and Palantir's not entirely running away with the Data Cloud market.

To close, like Snowflake, it's almost inevitable that Palantir experiences 5-10 years of share price stagnation as it layers on truly massive chunks of revenue in the same way Snowflake layered on truly massive chunks of revenue over the course of five years, at the end of which Snowflake's shares are still down about 50% from their late 2020 levels and are still not entirely obvious buys due to its 18x sales valuation and 25-30% growth.

Palantir's Brilliant Q1 '25

While Palantir's valuation will very likely be proven to be a case of irrational exuberance, I still love the business, and the business is firing on all cylinders and then some.

It's also worth noting that it's a 4% position in the LASR Index. And I'd be pleasantly surprised to see it grow at 75% annualized for five-ten consecutive years, which is what will be needed to justify its current valuation and create some ROI on the LASR Index's currently owned shares.

So, to these ends, let's review the stellar performance of the Palantir business that's presently underway.

We will start by analyzing customer count growth for both its commercial and government segments, then we will analyze the sales and profitability into which this is translating.

Let's begin.

Below, we can see that Palantir's total customer count continues to soar, growing 39% year over year, though Palantir's customer count growth did decelerate on a sequential basis.

Palantir's Total Customer Count

Palantir's Total Customer Count Growth Rate

Palantir needs to reaccelerate this metric in the coming quarters, else the 75% annualized growth rate it needs will be much more challenging to achieve.

That said, still phenomenal results here.

For commercial customers specifically, growth has been even better at 46%, though, like the total metric shared above, commercial customer count growth also decelerated sequentially.

Palantir Total Commercial Customer Count

Palantir Total Commercial Customer Count Growth

More deceleration here, which will need to change in the quarters ahead.

Turning to U.S. commercial customer count...

Palantir Total U.S. Commercial Customer Count

Palantir Total U.S. Commercial Customer Count Growth Rate

More deceleration as valuation continues to expand...

But, again, otherworldly growth here. Really phenomenal.

In short, Palantir's customer growth has been extraordinary, driven by its purpose-built AI platform, i.e., AIP on Foundry.

Let's now turn to a review of the sales into which the above customer growth has been translating.

Palantir Quarterly Commercial Revenue

Palantir Quarterly Commercial Revenue Growth Rate

This is fairly underwhelming, though still great, and it should accelerate in the quarters ahead as U.S. commercial becomes a larger part of the overall commercial business.

Palantir Quarterly U.S. Commercial Revenue

Palantir Quarterly U.S. Commercial Revenue Growth Rate

We have to hope that commercial accelerates to 100%+, then stays there for about a half decade, if not more. I mean it's possible, though the above-illustrated customer count growth must re-accelerate.

Turning to revenue, when I included Palantir in the LASR Index, my core thesis was that government would accelerate as AI was invariably adopted by the U.S. government, and this has played out.

This also reminds us that business is non-linear, and growth will fluctuate up and down throughout a business' lifecycle. We should not abandon ship when growth decelerates if the core thesis and product quality remain intact.

Palantir Quarterly U.S. Government Revenue Accelerates

Palantir Quarterly U.S. Government Revenue Growth Rate (Super Great)

In short, I couldn't be happier with government growth, and I do believe it will be a multi-ten billion dollar revenue business one day.

Turning to profitability...

As we can see, Palantir's free cash flow per share has been soaring as its revenue has grown and as its free cash flow margins have expanded.

Palantir Free Cash Flow Margin

Really great: Accelerating growth and expanding margins.

To close this section, Palantir now holds $5.4B in cash alongside $0 in debt.

Palantir Total Cash Balance

They have been wasting cash on a share buy back, which is useless at such a high valuation.

Let's now conclude this review by considering Palantir's guidance.

Concluding Thoughts

Below, I shared Palantir's guidance for Q2 '25.

Even with a sizable beat, e.g., sales come in at $975M, $40M above guidance, double what it beat by in Q1'25, growth would still only be 44%.

As we've covered: We need well above a 50% annualized growth rate for at least the next half decade to justify the current valuation.

Palantir Q2 '25 Guidance

To close:

  1. Palantir is an unbelievable business, and it's one of the most successful investments in the LASR Index. To this end, I hope it can achieve 50%+ annualized growth for the next 5-10 years so that this investment continues to succeed.
  2. Palantir's price action is virtually identical to that of Snowflake's in 2020/2021. This is painfully ironic because the software community, in 2020 and 2021, hated Palantir and loved Snowflake. The Snowflake champions were always hating on Palantir. Today, the roles have reversed, and so have their valuation dynamics.
  3. At the end of the day, I could be wrong in my assessment. That's possible. Palantir could grow at necessary, otherworldly rates to not only justify its valuation, but also to produce a healthy return for investors buying here (which I am de facto doing by holding the remaining shares in the LASR Index). That said, I am happy with what I've done with the position. I am happy with the sales and my caution here. At the end of the day, this is a game of probabilities, and Palantir was a high probability bet in the single digits, and it's now become a very low probability bet in at $130/share, up 1600%+ in just 2.5 years.

No matter what happens, I know I have managed this position with risk management in mind, and that's what is most important to me.

data.title: Northrop Grumman Vs. Lockheed Martin: Which Defense Prime Is The Better Buy?
data.description:
No matter your views on the current administration, it seems like everywhere you look, conflict (or the threat of it) is rearing its ugly head.

In Asia, long-standing border disputes between India and Pakistan — two nuclear-armed countries — threaten the local balance of power. Similarly, China’s interest in Taiwan represents a flashpoint that could spiral into a much larger war.

In Europe, Russia’s grinding war machine continues to take bites out of Ukraine, and in the Middle East, Israel continues trading blows with its neighbors.

Over here in the Americas, things have been relatively calmer, although border disputes on the northern coast of South America — along with Maduro — threaten regional stability and prosperity.

Conflict has existed forever, but over the last few years, it feels as though America’s general withdrawal from the global system has led to an uptick in regional violence.

Ultimately, conflict isn’t good for business; that is—unless you're a defense contractor. With the present economic situation, we'd argue that it makes sense to maintain a diversified portfolio of holdings that are exposed to global economic growth. However, with regional (and potentially global) conflict on the rise, buying into America’s military-industrial complex — as a hedge — might make sense.

In this article, we wanted to take a look at two prominent defense contractors: Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC).

Both of these firms produce the planes, missiles, space systems, and electronic warfare units that underpin much of the US military's current fighting power. With billions in revenues and significant bottom-line potential, both firms will likely act as a solid hedge in the event of wider global conflict.

But which stock is the better buy?

Today, we'll take a deeper look at LMT and NOC, explain each company's financial profile, and make the case that — ultimately — we think NOC appears to be the better buy for the long term.

Sound good? Let’s begin.

Financials

In terms of each company's financial performance, both LMT and NOC appear to be well-run, stable firms, operating and producing a diverse suite of critical weapons systems for the US military and our allies. Each firm boasts tens of billions in revenue, billions in operating income, and strong positive cash flows that can be reinvested into R&D and shareholder returns.

As both companies are defense prime contractors, they’re in the unique position of having a well-defined, stable customer base. This dynamic saves a lot of money on marketing and means that each stock's 'real' marketing efforts are actually in R&D. The better weapon systems and capabilities that each company can provide, the stronger the long-term sales performance.

Let’s start by taking a look at LMT, which is the larger of the two corporations.

Over the last 12 months, Lockheed produced $71.8 billion in revenue along with $7.4 billion in gross profit. This translates to a roughly ~10% gross profit margin. After subtracting out operating expenses, which are mostly negligible, LMT’s operating income also sits in the ~10% range:

Lockheed's relatively low — but stable — margins are the result of a 60/40 split in terms of overall contract pricing, with 40% of contracts being 'cost plus', and 60% being 'fixed price':

And just as a reminder, Kristine, 40% of our contracts are cost-type. And so what Evan's referring to mainly is the 60% that are fixed price, where we have those contractual clauses, both far-based and specially negotiated, that enable that recovery through different equitable adjustment means. So just wanted to make sure I added that as well.

As a reminder, on 'cost plus' contracts, LMT makes less profit, but also has much less margin volatility. These contracts happen with 'new' projects, where upfront costs are relatively unknown. This is where the military pays whatever LMT spends, plus an additional flat margin on top.

With 'fixed cost', the company has margin upside but takes on more risk.

Combined, with the firm's $180 billion backlog, it appears as though Lockheed's four critical divisions, including Aeronautics, Missions and Fire Control, Rotary and Mission Systems, and Space will continue turning out mid-single digit sales growth alongside consistent margin performance in the years ahead. This is underpinned by strong inertia in each segment over the last twelve months:

Shifting now to NOC, the company is materially smaller than LMT, only producing revenue of $40 billion over the last 12 months. However, the company's operating margins stand a bit higher, at roughly 12.2%:

This recent result fits in with NOC's average operating margin range of roughly 12% to 15% over the last decade.

Of note, this is higher than Lockheed’s 10% to 12.5% range.

Also of note is the fact that NOC's contract split — between fixed cost and cost plus — is closer to 50-50. This indicates that the company has done a better job squeezing margins out of a relatively lower-risk operating profile. Not bad.

Not only that, but NOC also makes different kinds of systems than Lockheed at the moment. Whereas LMT remains focused on tactical systems, like the F-35 Lightning II joint strike fighter, the F22 Raptor stealth fighter, and other platforms including HIMARS, Javelin, and THAAD, NOC is firmly focused on more strategic systems. These are platforms that are less useful in a direct fight but serve the country's interests more broadly. This includes things like the B21 Raider next-generation stealth bomber, Manta Ray underwater drone, and the IBCS for air and missile defense.

Between the two companies, Northrup and Lockheed produce a substantial portion of the US military’s overall air fighting power, but we actually prefer NOC's position within the defense stack. Sure, the military spends more on tactical fighters like the F35, but in terms of long-term strategic visibility, NOC's B21 raider — and other electronic warfare and satellite systems — make the company critically important in terms of strategic nuclear defense. As we see it, drones could easily disrupt the tactical weapons space, but there will always be a place for the nuclear triad and other 'big picture' weapons.

As NOC's results have already shown, this positioning provides a higher margin — and potentially longer tail — revenue source.

Analysts are also expecting NOC to grow revenues in the mid-single digit range, which is slightly higher than they're expecting for LMT:

For our part, taken together, we prefer NOC's overall financials and strategic positioning, although both companies remain largely performant.

Valuation

On the valuation front, both stocks sport similar multiples across financial metrics.

Presently, Lockheed Martin trades at 1.57x sales and 20x net income. Alternatively, Northrop trades at 1.74x sales and 19x net income. Not much of a disparity to write home about.

What’s interesting, though, is that NOC is currently trading in the lower half of its long-term price range, which indicates that it could be a potentially good time to buy into the stock:

Being in this 'discounted' range applies to both the stock's top and bottom-line multiples, which is encouraging. These ranges were constructed with a 250-week linear regression and 2x standard deviation bands. Being in the lower half of the range implies a 'deal' on the company's price—relative to organic performance.

This contrasts with LMT, which is trading in the lower half of the range with respect to sales but is not in terms of net income, rather, trading at around the midpoint:

This means that LMT—when looked at through the lens of the earnings multiple—is basically trading at an 'average' price.

It's not a 'bad' time to enter into the stock, but it's also not as potentially attractive as what you're getting with NOC.

With respect to the quant rating system, Seeking Alpha grades NOC a 'D+' valuation, while it grades LMT a 'C':

Normally, we tend to agree with SA's quant system, but in this case, we'd argue that the rating is wrong. Sure, Lockheed is a little bit cheaper, depending on which multiple you’re targeting, but relative to each company’s own multiple on a historical basis, there's no competition, NOC is trading at a much better price relative to itself. Some FCF distortions in recent periods for NOC have also jacked up some of these numbers, making them 'less bad' than feared.

Outlook

Finally, we come to the outlook, which is ultimately what will drive the lion's share of potential appreciation going forward.

On this front, we think that both companies have a strong future in defense contracting, but Northrop Grumman’s more focused efforts on autonomous systems, especially at sea and in the air, could win more military contracts in the future.

NOC's Global Hawk and Triton drones are advanced, high-altitude, long-endurance reconnaissance UAVs that should dominate the future battle space. The firm's knowledge and familiarity with drone technology should also position the firm well to target new weapons contracts. Right now, in China, many firms are thinking outside the box, including the Jiu Tian 'Mothership', which is a drone that carries other drones:

As we see it, NOC is well positioned to win potential contracts that travel along this vein, given their prior experience in the space.

Similarly, NOC's Manta Ray program—which focuses on uncrewed underwater vehicles—could be of tremendous interest as combat systems become more interconnected across domains.

All of these contrasts with LMT's approach to drone technology, which largely focuses on an 'integrated' approach. They're making sure that the fighters they sell are compatible with new drone tech in the future, but they're doing relatively little with new platforms right now.

On balance, we think NOC is more well positioned for the future of where we expect warfare to 'go'. This drone advantage is underscored by Ukraine's recent massive drone attack on Russian bombers, proving the battlefield value of drones:

Summary

Taken together, in our view, NOC has stronger margins, better strategic positioning, and a more advantageous outlook. Combined with a price point that we view as more attractive, it's hard to call NOC anything other than a 'Strong Buy' for the years ahead.

This doesn’t mean that LMT is a 'sell'. In fact, we're reiterating our 'Buy' rating on the company.

However, at this juncture, we prefer Northrop Grumman for those looking to bolster their portfolios and reduce geopolitical risk with a US prime defense contractor.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: TotalEnergies: A Buy-And-Hold Candidate With World-Class Discoveries
data.description:
Since the last article, TotalEnergies SE (NYSE:TTE) has beaten the overall market in terms of returns.

This performance is likely to continue in the long run because this company has some world-class discoveries that are significant even for a company of this size.

As management mentioned during the conference call, there are issues for the company to deal with as there is the threat of trade wars, tariffs, and of course, a very fluid situation that is changing rapidly.

For the time being, management has chosen to watch the situation closely for anything that requires action. But as was also noted during the conference call, this is a well-diversified company that is likely to overcome any challenges that arise from the current situation.

The key is to not panic. Rather remain calm and somehow eliminate the "noise" to get to the main actionable decisions to make in what is a negotiating process. It was discussed that there is a firm belief that whatever emerges will be constructive in the long run rather than destructive. That is something I happen to agree with. But it was also noted that it cannot be guaranteed.

There was also a discussion that if certain things would happen, there could be a material effect on some longer-term projects. However, there is also a belief that would not happen in the long run. Now let's see what happens.

Expectations

The oil and gas industry did not do all that well during the first Trump administration. There appears to be a market concern about how well the industry will do during a second administration. Total, as an international company, may have an advantage over the United States-based companies in a situation like this.

Those fears only got worse when the talk of WTI $50 oil emerged.

The reason is:

"But when it comes to justifying investments in new wells, respondents said they need at least $61 (in the Midland) to $70 in the ‘other’ parts of the Permian. The average price they need to profitably drill a new well is $65, up from $64 in early 2024. Firms producing at least 10,000 b/d have a cost advantage: Their leaders said they can drill at a WTI prices of $61/bbl while smaller producers said they need $66/bbl to expand."

The Dallas Federal Reserve conducted a survey of the industry that is worth reading. The quote above was summarized by coverage of that survey in the Oil & Gas Journal.

But something like this is how a reputation for hostility to the industry begins and concerns the market. Fears of industry underperformance show in a low valuation of oil and gas businesses relative to much of the market. Then, when a company like Total outperforms those low expectations, the stock price actually rises. Total has exposure to the United States oil and gas industry. But that is very different from being based in the United States as a company.

Things got further out of hand to the downside when exports to China concerning LNG products were now to be licensed. China was the reason a lot of LNG projects started in the first place. It was not the only reason. Therefore, it may be possible to profitably change strategies. Still, it is yet another example of a roadblock after money was spent on a particular project.

Earnings

Earnings came in a bit stronger than expected, despite weak commodity prices.

There were several questions about the climbing net debt. But this company had a big working capital jump after a quarter in which working capital went big in the other direction. While such fluctuations were a bit large for a company of this size, a couple of quarters does not mean that management has lost control of the situation (as the conference call questions seemed to imply). Instead, as management stated, what happened was well within management expectations.

So, it remains to be seen if things careen out of control. Acting against this market fear is the very high debt rating of the company. Very few companies achieve the debt rating that this company has.

The fear may have created a buying opportunity.

World-Class Projects

In the meantime, this company has some projects that individually could become significant "earners" even for a company of this size.

Below is a summary of both the Venus discovery in Namibia and probably another discovery that could be every bit as significant across the border in South Africa.

It was mentioned during the conference call that Namibia leasing terms were being discussed. Those negotiations could easily take up to a year. But those negotiations are likely to yield tangible (and viable) results.

In any event, the eventual production of these discoveries could turn out to be significant for the company and for shareholders.

Summary

Total Energies has a whole lot of projects in various stages of exploration and development. The above is likely the most visible example of all the projects underway. This company is likely in a position to outperform the industry for some time to come.

The combined return of the dividend and the stock price appreciation is likely to average into the teens for the long term. There may be some industrywide recovery potential as well. Many of these companies have very low price-earnings ratios when compared to the market average.

The investment risk for a company of this size with a very high debt rating is probably below average. But commodity prices could make the "ride" a bit bumpy at times.

This may well be a decent buy-and-hold candidate unless the story changes or the stock price gets too far ahead of itself.

Risks

The current political uncertainty is causing a lot of industries to pull back and wait for things to settle down. My own belief is what is going on is bargaining that will be replaced with something predictable and permanent that is constructive. But there is no assurance that will happen.

With a large company like this, the risk of any one project failing to meet expectations in such a way that it sets the company back is very small.

A large company usually has a deep bench. Therefore, the loss of any one person is seldom material to the company. But sometimes there is an unusually talented person whose loss of services could prove to be material to the company's prospects and hence a real challenge to replace.

data.title: China And Tariff Headwinds Could Cause General Motors To Break Down
data.description:
Thesis: Slipping sales and a deteriorating environment in China, along with tariff woes and potentially a softening American economy, put GM at risk, suggesting a sell rating.

I grew up outside of Detroit and have long admired General Motors Company (NYSE:GM), Ford Motor Company (F), and Chrysler from Stellantis N.V. (STLA). Throughout life, I’ve had a preference for American cars, a common sentiment among Michiganders. While the Big 3 have had their fair share of challenges and shortcomings, they also played a pivotal role in the development of the United States and the advancement of automotive technology. Yet as an analyst, I do my best to set aside any sentiments, and unfortunately, I believe GM is simply facing too many headwinds to justify it as a buy or even a hold right now.

China, Once an Engine of Profits, is Now a Potential Trap

General Motors was once a leader in the Chinese automotive industry, and for quite some time, China was a vital source of profits and revenue. Now, GM is facing declining sales in China. While GM is looking to turn things around, and actually enjoyed strong quarter-to-quarter growth in Q4 2024, the Chinese market is only going to get more competitive going forward. Chinese automaker BYD recently slashed prices by rather large amounts, potentially triggering a price war. GM may be forced to respond with price cuts of its own, or it could face further declining sales.

GM Sales Worldwide And By Segment - North America And Overseas

Unfortunately, it’s hard to see a path to turning sales around in China, especially compared to GM’s peaks back in 2017. China has been crucial not just for sales and revenues, but also for profits. This had plummeted to 8.6% in 2023, and sales have struggled since, declining 14%. The automaker is looking to turn things around with 10 new electric and hybrid models (ibid.).

Will a revamped lineup be enough? BYD recently slashed prices on many models, likely sparking a price war in the months ahead. Meanwhile, GM’s joint venture SAIC-GM expires in 2027 and will need to be renegotiated. With the ongoing trade war and tensions between China and the United States, it’s fair to wonder how those talks will go.

Too Many Eggs in a China Basket

Worse yet, GM has largely become a two-market pony, depending on the United States and China for most of its sales. GM once had a larger global footprint, but since China and the United States were the source of most of its profits, GM slimmed down to become more profitable overall.

Back in 2017, GM by and large exited the European market by selling off its Opel-Vauxhall business. GM had owned Opel-Vauxhall for nearly a century, but Europe had become a consistent source of losses. GM has exited many other markets, including Malaysia in 2018, Thailand in 2020, and Indonesia in 2015 (ibid.).

Focusing on profitable markets makes sense. The problem is that China is no longer profitable. In fact, in the first from January to September 2024, GM lost nearly $350 million on its China ventures. With domestic brands becoming increasingly competitive and ambitious, it’s hard to see a road by which GM can turn China into a major profit engine again.

GM might try to expand into other international markets once more, but with Chinese automakers set to flood unprotected markets, new entrances will prove very difficult. Some markets are protected from Chinese autos, like Europe, but BYD is already moving to set up manufacturing facilities across Europe and in Turkey (which won’t be subject to current EU tariffs). Some markets are protected from American auto imports as well.

Trump Tariff Bump May Cool Future Sales

The automotive industry is one of the most vulnerable industries to tariffs and trade wars. Even domestic manufacturers and foreign companies with extensive manufacturing facilities in the United States are at risk because parts are often sourced internationally. Many components come from Canada or Mexico, but China is a crucial source as well. Parts may also come from Japan, Europe, and various other markets. Below, you can see the content of some of the most “made in America” vehicles.

It should come as little surprise that consumers rushed to buy automobiles as the trade war started to heat up this past spring. Tariffs of 25% on Japanese and Korean automobiles will drive up the costs of popular Asian imports, which typically dominate the compact and medium car segment, while American manufacturers like GM typically do better with trucks and SUVs.

With prices set to rise, consumers flocked to car dealerships, and as a result, March and April turned out to be two of the strongest monthly performances on record. In March, 17.8 million vehicles were sold, marking a four-year high. Sales slipped slightly in April to 17.3 million, but historically, this was still a strong performance (ibid.). Ford did especially well, reporting a 16% YOY increase in April.

I believe that many households will have already stated their current demand for vehicles, which will produce a cooling effect in the months ahead.

Tariffs Will Hurt American Auto Companies Too

Meanwhile, tariffs on auto parts, steel, and various other inputs could drive up production costs in the United States. Tariffs on steel and aluminum were already set at 25% and are now set to rise to 50%. Even before the steel and aluminum hike, domestic car prices were expected to rise by about $3,000 for domestic autos.

Even if GM and other manufacturers want to reshore production, actually doing so would require years and massive investments. Given how capricious President Trump has been with tariffs, and the chance of future Presidents to undo many of the tariffs, I don’t think automakers will rush to reshore production, at least not for tariff reasons alone.

Of course, many tariffs may also end up struck down in the courts. If so, GM and other car companies could enjoy a nice short-term boost. If the American economy stabilizes, inflation remains low, and employment remains high, this could eventually translate into future car sales.

Consumers Falling Behind on Car Payments

Auto loan delinquencies have spiked in recent weeks, especially in the subprime segment, which has jumped to 6.6% of loans being 60 or more days behind. Prime loans have ticked up slightly as well, but so far, they don’t appear to be a major cause for concern. Yet, it’s possible the subprime jump could be a canary hinting at future problems for more fiscally well-off and responsible households.

VantageScore, which puts together a CreditGuage to monitor delinquencies and credit health, also found that auto delinquencies are on the rise. The company notes that student loan delinquencies are growing as well.

GM is on the hook for many auto loans through its financial services arm, GM Financial. The financial services arm set a quarterly record for originations in Q4 2024, totaling $15.5 billion, while full-year originations grew by 5.5% to reach $56 billion.

GM's Valuation Compared to Other Automakers

The auto industry is one of the most mature and more predictable markets. For valuation purposes, I want to focus on how GM stacks up with other automobile companies. The chart below shows GM's P/E ratio in recent years versus other automakers.

General Motors runs in the middle of the pack as far as P/E ratios are concerned. None of the companies above really stand out, although Hyundai's abnormally low P/E ratio would be worth investigating.

The tight P/E grouping is a crucial point for me: I don't think GM is poorly positioned or over-valued versus other automakers. In fact, having looked briefly at other automakers, I believe GM is in one of the better positions. I'd probably give Toyota Motor Corporation (TM) the edge because I believe they will cope better with declining sales in China, and it has a broader global footprint.

I also checked Seeking Alpha's quant tools for all six of the companies in the chart above. Five were ranked as holds, while Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF) is not currently covered by the quant tools. Seeking Alpha analysts give GM and Hyundai a buy rating, while the rest are considered holds.

Ultimately, my decision to rank GM as a sell is primarily due to my concerns over the general economy and the auto industry as a whole. Setting aside those concerns, GM would be a hold and I believe in and of itself the company is fairly valued based on its current fundamentals. Revenue growth is solid at 7.76%, but the net income margin is low at just ~3% versus a sector median of 4.36% and Toyota's healthy 9.92%.

GM also offers a 1.22% forward dividend yield, although there is a risk that they may cut dividends in the future, especially if China continues to slip. Seeking Alpha gives GM's dividend safety a D- rating, which strikes me as fair.

An upgrade to a buy rating would require me to see a path forward for growth that isn't dependent on China. GM is starting to emerge as a serious EV and hybrid competitor, and tariffs could make the American domestic market more generous for locally produced vehicles, so there may be roads to profitability to take. For now, however, my concerns regarding the auto industry weigh too heavily.

Takeaway: Between China and General Economic Concerns, General Motors is a Sell

Even if geopolitical tensions and the ongoing trade war weren’t a factor, GM’s prospects in China strike me as poor. Chinese automakers are ramping up, and future price wars will make it difficult for GM to drum up profits even if it can secure sales. Add in geopolitical tensions and the likelihood of rising nationalism, and the situation in China gets worse.

GM should continue to perform relatively well in the United States market. Yet between the tariff sales bump sating demand, signs of economic weakness, and the potential return of high inflation, I believe the American market could deteriorate. Consider that the ISM Services PMI, for example, fell to 49.9 in May, meaning the services sector contracted. Weakness in services could hint at larger problems in the economy overall, making auto companies and their big ticket items especially risky.

Still, the present risks in the American car market and the ongoing challenges in China dim GM’s prospects, and I suspect the company will struggle to match the performance of the S&P 500 and many other investment opportunities.

data.title: Not Without Risk: Why The BoC Is Signaling A Future Rate Cut Is Possible
data.description:
The Bank of Canada has held its key rate, but what would lead it to cut? MoneyTalk discusses with Andrew Kelvin, Head of Canadian and Global Rates Strategy with TD Securities.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - The Bank of Canada has once again held its key overnight rate. Governor Tiff Macklem is having to balance concerns about slowing economic growth with continued stickiness in inflation. Joining us now to discuss is Andrew Kelvin, Managing Director and Head of Canadian and Global Rate Strategy with TD Securities. Andrew, great to have you back on the program.

Andrew Kelvin - Thank you for having me. Always a pleasure.

Greg Bonnell - All right. Another rate decision. We're back here together again. Another hold after a streak of, what was it, seven cuts? What's going on? What's keeping them on hold?

Andrew Kelvin - So the June meeting was really a follow-through on the April meeting. In fact, if you look at their communiqué, their second to last paragraph, which is usually the forward-looking part of it, they usually end with some sort of vague statement and platitudes about our mission is to promote price stability or what have you, which is their mission. But we always go to the very bottom of the communiqués when they come out to see what they said in their forward looking component.

This time, they copy pasted it from April unchanged, which I think makes sense. Because I think from the Bank of Canada's perspective, not much has changed from April. When we were trying to put together our preview and collect our thoughts, one of the things we looked at is all the changes in the global trade front that had occurred.

And we'd gone from significant global trade frictions, to détente, to more trade frictions, to détente again. And then the cherry on top, after we'd already put our preview out, was an increase in steel and aluminum tariffs. And the Bank actually referred to that, and they just said, look, there's a lot of uncertainty here.

They can't be as forward looking as they previously had been. What that always implied was that they were going to need to really be a bit more reactive on the data as it came in. And if you look at the last, certainly, six or seven weeks of Canadian data, you're not going to find a clear signal on which way the economy is going.

So, absent a clear signal, the Bank of Canada's decision was to stay on hold, which I think is sensible, particularly given that it's a July 30 meeting next time. So it's quite late in July after being quite early in June. We will have quite a bit more data when they meet again in July. And, presumably, we'll have a bit more clarity as to what the global trade picture looks like as well, as well as what the uncertainty impacts are having in Canada.

Greg Bonnell - Well, as you dig into the statement, obviously, we just got that GDP report was stronger than expected. Some people may have said-- the casual observer may have said, but wasn't April inflation like at 1.7% below the Bank's target? Bank seems to be looking past that. They still seem to be concerned about inflation.

Andrew Kelvin - So that was, I think, to the extent there was something new we learned in this meeting. It wasn't so much that they're concerned about inflation, because we always know that the Bank of Canada is concerned about inflation. They are an inflation targeting central bank.

I think the thing I was struck by was that there may not be quite as much leeway in terms of how much inflation surprises they're willing to tolerate going forward. Now, headline inflation, you're right, it's below 2%. But that was on the consumer carbon tax being set to 0. That's a very easy shock to look through.

You know precisely why inflation fell that 6/10 of a percentage point. You know it will be in the inflation data for precisely 12 months. And you know it doesn't really reflect the underlying inflationary dynamic. And the same was true when carbon taxes were increasing.

The Bank would routinely look through those incremental tenth of a percentage point because that wasn't something they could control and not really something they should be trying to control here. So they're looking at the broader underlying price pressures. They're seeing that those are a little bit firmer than anticipated.

And I think it's important to note that, without knowing the result of the election, which was the case when the Bank of Canada came in April, both the leading parties, both the presumptive governing parties, were promising to set the consumer carbon tax to 0.

So that was already in their forecast. That was no surprise to them. And the fact that we still had an upside surprise on CPI relative to the previous forecasts, it did suggest to them that maybe inflation is a little bit firmer than they anticipated. And the thing I always go back to-- policymakers in general, global policymakers, they're always fighting the last battle.

The last thing a policymaker wants to do is not make the same mistake twice. You don't want to step on a rake and then immediately step on that very same rake.

Greg Bonnell - Saw a show like that once.

Andrew Kelvin - Yeah. Quite amusing.

So, for the Bank of Canada, the previous rake they stepped on was leaving policy rates too low in late 2021 and early 2022. I think with the benefit of hindsight, they probably would have started tightening in July 2021. And I think even without hindsight, there's a case for them to be tightening as early as October 2021.

And, as it stands, inflation was high for a longer period than they anticipated. Yes, some of it was global supply chain factors. Some of it was global demand due to global fiscal. But, certainly, the central bank probably could have done more with the benefit of hindsight.

Having gone through an inflationary episode, gotten inflation back down to about 2%, the last thing they want to do is cut too much in front of shocks that may not materialize and produce another inflationary spike. That is how inflation expectations ultimately become unanchored. So that's what they're trying to avoid here.

And I think if there's a message I got from Governor Macklem today that was a new message-- the governor was asked-- I'm going to paraphrase a bit here-- but the governor was asked if inflation needed to move lower in the next two months for there to be a cut in July.

Now, he's never going to answer these questions, yes or no. The one thing the Bank of Canada has come to understand is optionality is their friend. They never want to paint themselves into a corner.

Greg Bonnell - Never say something, if you're buying a house, starting a business, getting a big loan, don't worry, rates will be low for a long time-- something like that.

Andrew Kelvin - Yeah, something like that that hems them in has been, in the past, not something that's worked out especially well for them. So I think they've done a really good job in this cycle about being very forthright about what they know, what they don't know, and trying to keep options on the table for as long as possible.

So when the governor is asked if inflation needs to come lower to cut rates, he's never going to be like, yes, or he's never going to say no because there's more to it, then there's so many moving parts. They don't know what the things outside the very narrow inflation data that could happen in the next eight weeks, because just look at the course of 2025.

So much of what's happened was unpredictable that they, again, don't want to get pinned in the corner. Having said all that-- I'm taking a long time to get to this point, I recognize that. There is a point, I promise.

When he was asked about it, rather than dismissing it and saying it will depend on a whole host of factors, he did dive into a fairly long discussion about how inflation has firmed to a greater extent than they expected. They talked about ex tax inflation, how their core measures were a bit firmer than they had been a few months previously. It was a long explanation. And while he didn't give a yes/no, it felt decisively like not a no, to put it another way.

Greg Bonnell - There's a lot of uncertainty, obviously, with our largest trading partner. We do have the certainty now as to who has formed the government-- Prime Minister Mark Carney and the Liberals. But we're not going to budget. So what does the Bank of Canada make of that?

Do they need some fiscal clarity? We have promises about Canada being a energy superpower, about new construction of infrastructure. But what does the Bank do with that information until they see a budget?

Andrew Kelvin - So the governor was asked this. And his line, which I think is appropriate, was that uncertainty around Canadian fiscal policy is not the biggest source of uncertainty the Bank of Canada is facing here.

Greg Bonnell - It's not what keeps them awake at night?

Andrew Kelvin - It's not what's keeping them awake at night. The Bank has a very simple process here. They will put fiscal measures into their projections once those measures are announced and legislated.

They are not going to start trying to anticipate the changes a new government will make. And when you talk about things like making Canada an energy superpower, these are not overnight changes. These are, perhaps, not changes that can occur within a monetary policy window, which is typically thought of as six to eight quarters, or even a single electoral mandate.

Having said all that, if you look at the Liberal platform, there are some measures in it that will have more short-term economic impacts, tax cuts front of mind for that. Once those are enacted and start to flow through into the economy, the bank will reflect that in their forecasts. So when I think about what the later budget does to the way we're thinking about the Bank of Canada path, you have this window in the summer where the House is no longer in session, we, presumably, haven't seen a budget.

I've seen no indication that we're going to have a budget rushed in before the House rises for the summer. And you have this period where you haven't seen significant new fiscal stimulus into the forecast. Now, the need for it now is probably a little bit less than what we would have feared. The government did acknowledge today that the economy seems to veering more towards the more benign scenario that they'd imagined, rather than the more severe one.

But what that does is it opens up a period where we have two more quarters where the economy is going to be mostly operating under the influence of external factors-- under the impacts of what the trade tensions are going to be doing, the changing demographics, the monetary stimulus already in the system. It's an economy that's not going to be getting that boost from the government for the next few quarters.

That's the most likely window for the BOC to be able to cut. If we get to the October meeting and we're still having this conversation about how they're waiting for weakness to show up, I think a reasonable conclusion is that the weakness isn't going to be coming to the same extent that, perhaps, we had feared. So when I think about this sort of probabilistically, I look at Q3 as a period where rate cuts are far more plausible than, let's say, the first quarter of 2026.

Greg Bonnell - When do they start? Quickly, I'll ask you-- what do you got baked in the forecast right now? When do they get off the sidelines again?

Andrew Kelvin - So we have three more cuts on our forecast. We have cuts in July, September, and October. By the time you get to December, I think it should be reasonably apparent what the damage done from trade uncertainty is. I'm sure there will be some.

The magnitude is really hard to determine, but I think by December, whatever the economy looks like due to those trade tensions, it should be mostly reflected in activity. And by the time we hit that December meeting, you're now getting that Q4 data. You now have an idea of what is going to be coming on the fiscal policy side of things. And we will have three more cuts in our forecast, so you'll have moved from a neutral policy setting to a slightly accommodative one.

At that point, we don't think anything more will be needed. And it really just comes down to a question of, how can the Canadian economy weather things through the summer? We see, essentially, zero growth over Q2 and Q3. If I want to put my optimist hat on, I can find some reasons to think that maybe there will be things coming that we, perhaps, do not anticipate.

A really interesting conversation for me, which I think probably a lot of our viewers are having themselves, is around what happens with vacation spending. We have seen these indications that Canadians traveling to the US, those numbers have started to fall off a little bit. I'm not a huge believer in this notion that Canadians can wholesale replace their imported consumption baskets with domestic ones.

We import 30% to 35% of GDP. I think it's 32%, 33% of GDP. To replace that with domestic sources is probably not plausible. But if there's one big ticket item that Canadians do tend to spend abroad and can be easily substituted domestically, it is travel.

Some of that travel spending that had previously gone to the US will be saved. It just won't wind up in the economy. Some of it will be rerouted to other non-Canadian but non-US destinations. Some of it will go to the US. If you've promised you're taking your kids to Disney World, you may well still be taking your kids to Disney World. There's not a good substitute for that domestically.

But some of that will be redirected domestically. There will be Canadians who, perhaps, have been thinking about going to the US, but given that the currency is-- well, Canadian dollar has appreciated a little bit. The exchange rate is still not especially favorable for Canadian travelers, given that maybe there is some sentiment changes in terms of a buy Canada theme and buy Canada narrative-- we could see a little bit more domestic tourism this summer, which would be an upside risk to our forecast.

All told, we have zero growth. If that materializes, we think we can get three more cuts. If it looks a little bit better than that, the case for cutting is just simply not as compelling.

Original Post

data.title: Tandem Diabetes Care: A Barbell Bet On A High-Stakes Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Tandem Diabetes Care, Inc. (NASDAQ:TNDM) was nearly two years ago today. The medical device company known for its automated insulin delivery (AID) systems was struggling to begin 2023, mired by declining pump shipments, narrowing gross profits, and widening net losses. Still, Tandem was hopeful for 10%-plus growth and international expansion. My recommendation was a “cautious Hold.” Its stock has since declined 22.68% while the S&P 500 has rallied 36.73%.

The following article reveals a mixed financial performance for Tandem, but there are reasons to hold for risk-tolerant investors.

Financials

Upon a glance at Tandem’s financial data, it’s plain to see why its stock has underperformed. Revenues tanked nearly 7% in 2023, far from its optimistic estimates. Notably, the company did turn things around in 2024, growing revenues 25.7% year-over-year to reach an all-time high of $940 million. However, its 2024 performance did not rally its stock. Although its Q4 2024 earnings report featured notable beats on EPS ($0.01) and revenue ($282.6 million), its 2025 guidance worried the market. Tandem guided for between $997 million and $1.007 billion in revenues, which appeared soft to analysts.

Fast-forward to Q1 2025, Tandem generated $234.4 million in worldwide sales, compared to $191.67 million in Q1 2024. Pump shipments in the U.S. increased by 19%. However, there were a few financial events that contributed to a widening net loss of $130.6 million, including $75.217 million in acquired in-process R&D expenses, $6.697 million in non-recurring facility impairment costs, and $4.47 million in restructuring costs.

Notably, there were meaningful improvements in gross profit and Adjusted EBITDA relative to last year. Tandem reaffirmed its 2025 guidance.

Recent Developments

In the AID system market, Tandem’s Control-IQ+ competes with Medtronic plc's (MDT) MiniMed 780G, Insulet’s Omnipod 5, Beta Bionics iLet Bionic Pancreas, and Sequel’s twiist. The latter was introduced last year. Each system has its idiosyncrasies, and no one is “better” than the other. The core functionalities are similar. Tandem’s unique feature is that it has a predictive algorithm and an extended bolus of up to eight hours. Insulet is tubeless. And so on.

The problem is that Tandem is tasked with competing with healthcare conglomerates like Medtronic. So, it’s challenging for smaller players like Tandem to balance heavy innovation investments with limited funding. Tandem has no option but to take on debt.

While we’re on the subject, as of March 31, Tandem had $368.6 million in cash and short-term investments. It has $40.7 million in “current portion of long-term debt.” This is debt that is due within the next 12 months. Tandem’s long-term debt is $313.6 million. In Q1, Tandem “burned” $18.3 million (cash from operations). Assuming a similar cash burn rate moving forward, Tandem’s cash and investments afford it approximately five years of cash runway. However, this doesn’t account for Tandem’s upcoming debt obligations. Most of Tandem’s convertible senior notes are due in 2029. So, Tandem has time to sort things out, and it believes it can achieve free cash flow positivity in the second half of 2025.

On the innovation front, Tandem’s AID is undergoing a few changes. It intends to get rid of the tubing in the Mobi platform. The algorithm is evolving, and Tandem hopes to achieve fully closed-loop technology. Extended wear infusion technology aims to provide longer-lasting infusion sets. There are connectivity improvements, like expanding smartphone control capabilities. This is all very tedious, but it’s required to stay ahead in a market characterized by many similar products. Many of these improvements target convenience.

Tandem’s Control-IQ+ was approved for type 2 diabetes in February 2025, which more than doubles its addressable market. For those who chose the computer class over anatomy and physiology in high school, most patients with type 2 diabetes still produce their insulin and, thus, don’t require exogenous insulin from an AID. However, it has been estimated that 12.3% of diabetes patients need exogenous insulin within a year of diagnosis. So, Tandem will focus on patients with a high insulin burden. Tandem also remains focused on international expansion. In Q1, international sales grew 35% year-over-year.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on TNDM. The purpose of doing this is to play “What If?” The company’s long-term financial goals are 25% operating margins and 60% gross margins by 2026. So, let’s assume the company achieves cash flow positivity and maintains revenue growth at 10%, on average, over the nine-year projection period.

In such a case, TNDM is undervalued by 38.25%. However, be aware that the above projection is an “assumption within an assumption.” Although the company believes it can generate cash, it has yet to prove this.

Tandem is highly sensitive to growth and margin assumptions.

The sensitivity analysis that follows centers on our Base scenario.

TNDM is a stock with substantial upside if management executes on its goals. But this is a big "if," and the upside is not asymmetric. The company has a long history of operating losses. Its operating expenses remain high. There is intense competition. On the other hand, it is growing revenue at a strong pace. Gross margins are improving.

Conclusion

To wrap up, Tandem is seeing financial improvements, but the market remains very cautious. And I cannot blame them. While there is considerable upside should Tandem exceed conservative expectations, there is also substantial downside (90%-plus) should companies like Medtronic continue to eat Tandem’s lunch. So, TNDM only makes sense in a barbell portfolio, where allocation is limited to offset the potential for substantial losses (maintain a Hold rating). With that said, a turnaround could be near (the achievement of consistent free cash flow), and TNDM is worth watching.

data.title: Currency Coordination Looks Riskier Today
data.description:
By Victor Xing

The Taiwan dollar’s rapid appreciation in the second quarter led to speculation of a “Plaza Accord 2.0” — a coordinated effort to weaken the US dollar — echoing the historic 1985 agreement among G5 nations. The original Plaza Accord was designed to address large US trade deficits by engineering a controlled depreciation of the dollar through joint currency intervention. It marked a rare and powerful example of global currency coordination.

Any new Plaza-style agreement today would face far greater financial and geopolitical hurdles than it did 40 years ago. Indeed, if US policymakers seek to stimulate domestic manufacturing by depreciating the dollar, they must also account for the emerging costs and risks associated with global trade, capital flows, and market stability.

This post examines the potential consequences of a coordinated dollar depreciation today — from FX volatility and insurance risk to broader macroeconomic impacts.

A Weaker Dollar Could Heighten Global FX Volatility

A weaker US dollar could have a dramatic effect on the FX market and, specifically, on Taiwanese life insurance companies. A January 2025 FT article pointed out that these companies hold assets equivalent to 140% of Taiwan’s GDP. A substantial portion of these holdings are in US-dollar-denominated bonds only partially hedged for FX volatility.

Taiwan has enjoyed widening current account surpluses due in large part to strong demand for its semiconductors. To manage the resulting FX reserve growth and to maintain FX stability, the local monetary authority permitted life insurance companies to swap their Taiwan dollars for US dollars in the FX reserve. The insurers then swapped USD to buy US fixed-income assets to meet future (insurance policy) obligations.

Despite shifting the bulk of their portfolio assets to US dollars, most of the insurance policies (firm liabilities) remain denominated in local currency. The result would be a significant currency mismatch where sharp declines in the US dollar would reduce the value of US-dollar-denominated bonds such as US Treasuries held by Taiwanese insurance companies, leaving the insurance companies with insufficient assets to match their liabilities.

The original Plaza Accord signed by the G-5 countries in 1985 was agreed upon under the backdrop of a relatively benign macro environment. A hypothetical “Plaza Accord 2.0” to depreciate the US dollar would likely increase pressure on Taiwan’s insurers and their risk-management efforts. This vicious cycle would exacerbate pressure and magnify FX market volatility.

Taiwanese insurance companies are also exposed to duration risks. The US dollar bonds held by Taiwanese insurance companies are longer-duration (with greater interest rate sensitivity than short-maturity debt). Sales of these assets would likely lift long-term US interest rates and transmit interest rate volatility across markets.

Taiwanese insurers are not alone in their exposure to this type of risk. Similar carry-trade flows (sell local currency, buy US dollar and dollar-denominated assets) with the Japanese yen in the third quarter of 2024 triggered a brief-but-disruptive volatility surge across major asset markets.

The US Trade Deficit’s Hidden Role

A “Plaza Accord 2.0” coming 40 years after the original accord would need to account for the US trade deficit as part of a circular currency flow to fund the US government. In 1985, the US deficit was at $211.9 billion. By 2024 it had risen to $1.8 trillion. Similarly, the US debt ballooned from $1.8 trillion in 1985 to $36.2 trillion in the second quarter this year. Non-US exporters reinvesting trade surplus dollars in US Treasuries (lending surplus dollars back to the US government) are a key source of liquidity in the US bond market:

Under the present paradigm, a lower US trade deficit would likely disrupt the reinvestment of exporter dollar trade surpluses, which could reduce foreign demand at US Treasury auctions and negatively affect secondary market liquidity conditions.

“Plaza Accord 2.0’s” Nuanced Impact On a Leaner US Manufacturing Sector

The US manufacturing sector has evolved significantly over the past 40 years. According to BEA data, the US manufacturing sector’s share of nominal GDP fell to 9.9% in 4Q 2024 from 18.5% in 1985.The total number of workers in the manufacturing sector also declined. In April 1985, manufacturing employees as a share of total non-farm payrolls was 18.4%. By April 2025, that number had dropped to 8.0%. The reduction in manufacturing headcount (with improved productivity, until gains began to stagnate in the late 2000s) implies US manufacturing had become more efficient between 1987 and 2007:

Thus, a changed manufacturing industry with relatively smaller payrolls now than in 1985 would likely benefit differently from impacts of Plaza style accords than four decades ago, when more households were directly participating in the industry.

Assessing the Risk Reward of “Plaza Accord 2.0”

Studies on the impact of the original Plaza Accord concluded that exchange rate shifts ultimately led to changes in trade balances with a lag of two years. A similar lag would likely apply today, raising questions about whether a new Plaza-style intervention could meaningfully support US manufacturing — now a leaner, smaller share of GDP — without triggering broader financial disruptions. Compared to 1985, today’s global system is more interconnected and more reliant on the dollar, particularly through foreign holdings of US debt. Any coordinated effort to weaken the dollar would need to balance potential industrial gains against risks to FX stability, institutional asset-liability mismatches, and the functioning of US debt markets. The cost-benefit equation for “Plaza Accord 2.0” is far more complex than it was four decades ago.

Calls for a “Plaza Accord 2.0” reflect growing concern over US trade imbalances and industrial competitiveness. But unlike in 1985, the global economy today is more complex, with deeper interdependencies and more fragile financial linkages. A new Plaza-style agreement would carry unintended consequences — from FX volatility and insurance-sector risk in Asia to disruptions in US debt financing and monetary policy transmission.

Under the original Plaza Accord, currency shifts took years to influence trade balances, underscoring the lag between intervention and impact. Policymakers must therefore assess whether the benefits to a leaner US manufacturing base would outweigh the risks to global markets, institutional stability, and US fiscal operations. In this environment, the risk-reward calculus of currency coordination looks far more complicated than it did 40 years ago.
